linkedin_post:
Building reliable spatial awareness in robotics often hits a wall when you rely solely on monocular vision. Robots need to identify not just what an object is, but exactly how far away it sits, without the luxury of specialized depth sensors.

To address this, I developed a real-time object detection and distance estimation system designed for edge environments. The architecture integrates an Ultralytics YOLOv8n model—fine-tuned on the BDD100K dataset—with a geometric distance estimation module. By calculating distance based on bounding box dimensions, focal length, and known object width (D = (W*f)/P), the system provides actionable spatial data using only standard camera input.

A significant challenge during development was balancing detection accuracy with the computational constraints of edge devices. Accurate distance estimation relies heavily on precise camera calibration and realistic object width assumptions. When the environment changed—such as shifts in lighting or occlusion—the accuracy of the bounding boxes fluctuated, which directly impacted the distance calculation. I addressed this by optimizing the model backbone and exporting to ONNX, which significantly reduced inference latency on CPU, ensuring the pipeline remained stable enough for real-time applications.

Working through this project taught me that the bottleneck in computer vision isn't always the model performance itself; it is often the integration of geometric algorithms with deep learning outputs. Designing a modular codebase in Python allowed me to keep the detection and distance logic decoupled, which made the system much easier to test and benchmark.

Moving forward, I am interested in how we might handle depth estimation in more dynamic or cluttered environments where object size is highly variable. 

How are you approaching distance estimation in your computer vision projects? Do you lean toward monocular geometric methods or multi-modal sensor fusion?
